palette.name: Word brand blue (accent)
palette.usage: Primary Word accent. App theme color used for active-tab underline/indicator, File-tab and ribbon-launcher accents, focus rings, hyperlink-style highlights, and the Word app icon. RGB(43,87,154), CSS name 'Endeavour'.

palette.name: File tab (Backstage) blue
palette.usage: The 'File' tab fill in the tab strip (a solid Word-blue chip), and the Backstage left rail uses this same brand blue.

palette.name: Ribbon / window background
palette.usage: Default neutral ribbon body and overall chrome background in the modern (post-2019/M365) light theme. Very light warm gray.

palette.name: Tab strip background
palette.usage: Background behind the ribbon tab labels (Home, Insert, ...). In the flat modern theme it matches the ribbon background; the active tab is distinguished by its underline, not a different fill.

palette.name: Active tab background
palette.usage: Selected ribbon tab body. In modern M365 the selected tab text is brand blue with a ~2px #2B579A underline; the ribbon body below reads as white/near-white.

palette.name: Active tab indicator (underline)
palette.usage: ~2px solid brand-blue underline beneath the currently selected tab label.

palette.name: Inactive tab text
palette.usage: Unselected tab labels and most ribbon/group label text. Near-black neutral (#444 to #333).

palette.name: Button hover background
palette.usage: Hover state fill for ribbon buttons, gallery items, and tabs. Light neutral gray (Fluent neutralLight).

palette.name: Button pressed / selected background
palette.usage: Pressed/active and toggled-on (e.g. Bold engaged) ribbon button fill. Slightly darker gray (Fluent neutralQuaternaryAlt). Toggled-on buttons also tint subtly toward brand blue.

palette.name: Button hover/pressed border
palette.usage: 1px subtle border that appears around a button on hover/press (Fluent neutralTertiaryAlt).

palette.name: Group divider / separator
palette.usage: 1px vertical hairline separating ribbon groups, and the thin rule above group labels. Also used for QAT and status-bar separators.

palette.name: Document canvas (work area) gray
palette.usage: The gray area surrounding the page in the editing view (classic ~#ECECEC to #E6E6E6). The page 'floats' on this neutral gray.

palette.name: Page (paper) white
palette.usage: The document page itself - pure white sheet with a soft drop shadow against the canvas gray.

palette.name: Page drop shadow
palette.usage: Soft shadow under/around the page giving the floating-paper effect (e.g. 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.2)).

palette.name: Selection highlight blue
palette.usage: Text selection highlight in the document when the window is focused (light Windows selection blue, approx #ADD6FF to #CCE8FF). Inactive/out-of-focus selection falls back to a light gray.

palette.name: Status bar background
palette.usage: Bottom status bar fill in the modern light theme (neutral). Earlier 'Colorful' builds painted the status bar in Word-blue #2B579A with white text; the current M365 default is the neutral light bar with dark text.

palette.name: Status bar text
palette.usage: Status-bar text and icons (page count, word count, language, zoom). Near-black neutral on the light bar.

palette.name: Title bar background
palette.usage: Modern M365 title bar / QAT region is near-white. (Legacy 'Colorful' theme used Word-blue #2B579A title bar with white caption text and white QAT icons.)

palette.name: Ribbon icon color (monoline)
palette.usage: Fluent monoline ribbon glyphs render in a dark neutral (#424242/#444), with selective brand-blue or category accents on a few icons.

typography.spec: Segoe UI (Windows). Fallback stack: 'Segoe UI', 'Segoe UI Web', system-ui, Tahoma, Arial, sans-serif. Antialiased/ClearType. Weights used: Regular 400, Semibold 600, Bold 700.
Segoe UI Regular, ~12px (9pt). Centered in modern M365; near-black on white (or white on blue in legacy Colorful).
Segoe UI Regular, ~14px (12px-14px range). Selected tab is brand blue #2B579A; unselected #444. Comfortable letter spacing, sentence case.
Segoe UI Semibold, ~14px, white text on the #2B579A blue chip.
Segoe UI Regular, ~12px (9pt). Two-line wrap allowed under large buttons (e.g. 'Format\nPainter'). Color #444.
Segoe UI Regular, ~11px (9pt). Centered under each group (Clipboard, Font, Paragraph, Styles, Editing). Color #444/#605E5C neutral.
Segoe UI Regular, ~12px. Style names in the Styles gallery render in their own formatting (e.g. Calibri 11) but the chrome chrome around them is Segoe UI.
Segoe UI Regular, ~12px (9pt). 'Page 1 of 1', 'X words', language, zoom %. Color #444.
Segoe UI Regular ~12px body with Segoe UI Semibold ~12-13px title.
Calibri 11pt (the Office default body font). Line spacing 1.08, 8pt space-after paragraph (Office default). Calibri is a humanist sans-serif; web fallback: Calibri, 'Carlito', 'Segoe UI', sans-serif.
Calibri Light (Headings) for Heading 1/Title in the default Office theme; body style uses Calibri. (Newer 'Aptos' theme ships in latest M365, but Calibri remains the classic default referenced here.)

metrics.value: ~28-32px. Shares the title-bar band in M365; icons ~16px in a ~24px hit target.
~28-30px (tab label row), with ~2px active-tab underline at its base.
~92px (range 90-95px). Total ribbon block (tabs + body) is roughly 120-125px tall when expanded.
~52-60px for a small group (e.g. Editing) up to ~120-200px for large groups (Font, Paragraph). Groups scale/collapse responsively; collapsed group = single ~52px button with dropdown.
~48px tall x ~36-44px wide (icon ~32px + two-line label). Small buttons ~22px square hit targets in a 3-row stack.
1px vertical hairline (#E1DFDD) with ~4-6px padding either side between groups.
~18-20px strip below the ribbon when View > Ruler is on; matching ~18px vertical ruler at left.
~16-17px (Windows standard); thin overlay ~8px in newer builds.

metrics.element: Page size - US Letter (default, US locale)

metrics.value: 1 in (2.54 cm) on all four sides (Normal margins). At 96 px/in => 96px each side. Text column on Letter = 816 - 192 = 624px wide.

metrics.element: Default page gap (between pages in view)
metrics.value: ~10-14px gray gutter between consecutive page sheets in Print Layout.
Soft drop shadow approx 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.20) around each white sheet on the gray canvas.

notes: Word brand blue confirmed as #2B579A (RGB 43,87,154, CSS name 'Endeavour'). It is the accent used for the File tab chip, active-tab underline, focus rings, and the legacy Colorful-theme title/status bars. Source: brandcolorcode.com, usbrandcolors.com, colorhexa.

Default UI font is Segoe UI across all chrome (tabs, group labels, button captions, status bar). Default DOCUMENT font is Calibri 11pt with 1.08 line spacing and 8pt space-after (Office defaults). Newest M365 builds ship 'Aptos' as the document default, but Calibri 11 is the classic/long-standing default this spec targets.

Theme distinction: The MODERN M365 default ('Colorful' in current builds) uses a flat near-white/neutral chrome (#F3F2F1 ribbon, near-white title bar) with brand-blue accents only on the File tab and active-tab underline. The OLDER 'Colorful' theme (2013-2019) painted the entire title bar AND status bar solid Word-blue #2B579A with white text/icons. State both so the implementer can pick.

Active tab styling in modern Word: NOT a raised/colored tab. The selected tab shows brand-blue label text plus a ~2px #2B579A underline; the ribbon body below it reads as white. Inactive tabs are #444 text on the neutral strip.

Button states (Fluent neutrals): rest = transparent on ribbon bg; hover = #E1DFDD fill with 1px #C8C6C4 border and ~2-3px rounded corners; pressed/selected/toggled-on = #D2D0CE fill (toggled-on like engaged Bold also gets a subtle blue tint). Corner radius on ribbon controls is small (~2-3px); the chrome is largely flat/squared per Fluent.

Group structure: each ribbon group is a cluster of controls with a centered ~11px Segoe UI caption at the bottom (Clipboard | Font | Paragraph | Styles | Editing on the Home tab). Groups are separated by a 1px vertical hairline (#E1DFDD). Many groups have a small dialog-launcher arrow at the bottom-right that opens the full dialog.

Icons are Fluent monoline glyphs (single-weight outlines) rendered in dark neutral #424242/#444, a few with category accents (e.g. text-highlight yellow, font-color red bar). They are crisp at 16px and 32px. Avoid filled/skeuomorphic icons - that's the pre-2013 look.

Document canvas: white page (#FFF) with soft drop shadow floating on a light gray work area (~#E6E6E6). Default view is Print Layout showing the full sheet with 1in margins; text selection highlights light blue (~#CCE8FF) when focused.

Rounded corners overall: the chrome is predominantly flat/squared (Fluent). Only minor 2-3px rounding on hover/pressed button backgrounds, tooltips, and dropdown popovers; the window itself follows the OS (Windows 11 adds ~8px rounded window corners).
Separators & dividers summary: vertical group dividers (1px #E1DFDD), QAT-to-title separators, and status-bar item separators are all thin 1px neutral hairlines; nothing uses heavy/dark borders.

features.name: Inline character formatting (bold, italic, underline, strikethrough, sub/superscript, color, highlight, font family, font size)
features.rationale: contenteditable maps directly to inline spans/CSS. These are the cheapest possible wins and round-trip cleanly to OOXML run properties (w:rPr).
features.approach: Use a wrapped command layer (own selection->Range manipulation, not the deprecated document.execCommand, though execCommand works for an MVP). Represent formatting as <span> with inline styles or class-based marks. Map to w:b, w:i, w:u, w:color, w:highlight, w:sz, w:rFonts on export via the 'docx' npm library.

features.name: Paragraph alignment, indentation, line spacing, space before/after
features.rationale: All expressible as CSS on block elements and as w:pPr (w:jc, w:ind, w:spacing) in OOXML.
features.approach:
Apply CSS to

/

blocks; serialize to ParagraphProperties. Watch unit conversion: OOXML uses twips (1/1440 inch) and EMUs; build a units helper.

features.name: Tabs and custom tab stops (leaders, decimal tabs)
features.rationale: Browser does not honor Word-style tab stops; the Tab key in contenteditable does not create true tab stops with leaders or decimal alignment. CSS has no decimal-tab.
features.approach: For basic left tab stops, insert tab characters rendered with tab-size or padded spans. Decimal/leader tabs require a custom measurement pass (canvas text metrics) to position content; doable but laborious. Store tab definitions in model and approximate visually; round-trip the w:tabs definitions faithfully even if rendering is approximate.

features.name: Text effects, WordArt, shadows/glow/reflection on text
features.rationale: CSS text-shadow / -webkit-text-stroke / gradients can fake most effects visually, but WordArt is a DrawingML construct (txbx) that will not round-trip without serializing the full drawing XML.
features.approach: Implement visually with CSS for the editor. For round-trip, either drop to plain styled text on export or generate minimal DrawingML. Treat full WordArt fidelity as out of scope.

features.name: Character spacing / kerning / ligatures / OpenType features
features.rationale: letter-spacing and font-feature-settings exist in CSS, but Word's per-point spacing/kerning controls and the exact metrics differ from the browser's text shaping.
features.approach: Expose letter-spacing and font-feature-settings; map to w:spacing(letterSpacing)/w:kern. Exact metric parity with Word's renderer is not achievable.

features.name: Paragraph & character styles (named styles, Normal/Heading 1-9, style inheritance)
features.rationale: Maps naturally to CSS classes + a style table. OOXML styles.xml is a well-understood part and the 'docx' library supports defining styles.
features.approach: Maintain a style registry; render via a scoped stylesheet with class names; on export emit styles.xml entries and apply pStyle/rStyle references. Implement basedOn inheritance by CSS cascade or by resolving the chain in the model.

features.name: Linked styles, table styles, list styles
features.rationale: Paragraph/character/linked styles are tractable. Table styles (banded rows, conditional first-row formatting) and list styles are more complex but representable.
features.approach: Implement paragraph/character/linked first. Table styles: store the styleId and render banding via CSS nth-child; full conditional formatting (tblStylePr per region) is partial. Round-trip the styleId even when rendering is simplified.

features.name: Themes (theme colors, theme fonts, color schemes)
features.rationale: A theme is just a palette + font pair + effect set (theme1.xml). Colors and fonts are easy; effect themes and the full theme switching that recolors everything is more work.
features.approach: Implement theme color/font tokens as CSS variables; a theme switch re-binds variables. Export theme1.xml with the color scheme and font scheme. Effect themes (subtle/moderate/intense shape effects) are out of scope.

features.name: Style sets, Style Inspector, Reveal Formatting
features.rationale: These are UI conveniences. Reveal-formatting (showing computed formatting at the cursor) is straightforward with getComputedStyle; style sets (swapping the whole quick-style gallery) require multiple predefined style tables.
features.approach: Reveal Formatting: read computed styles + model props at selection. Style sets: ship a few predefined style tables and swap. Cheap to do partially.

features.name: Page size, margins, orientation (model + export)
features.rationale: These are document/section properties (w:sectPr: pgSz, pgMar). Storing and round-tripping them is easy; the difficulty is only in faithful on-screen pagination.
features.approach: Store section properties; render a page-width container with CSS @page-like padding for the active section; export sectPr accurately.

features.name: True WYSIWYG pagination (line-accurate page breaks, page count, page-fill flow across pages)
features.rationale: This is THE hard problem of the contenteditable approach. The browser flows content in one continuous box; it has no concept of pages. Replicating Word's line-breaking/widow-orphan/keep-with-next layout engine to compute identical page breaks is a multi-month layout-engine effort, not a one-session task. CSS paged media / Paged.js can fake static pagination but breaks editing (caret/selection across page boxes) and still won't match Word's algorithm.
features.approach: For an MVP, render as a single continuous 'web layout' page (Word has this mode too). Optionally overlay decorative page-break markers. Do NOT promise Word-identical page geometry.

features.name: Manual page breaks and section breaks
features.rationale: Inserting an explicit break marker is trivial in the model and maps to w:br(page)/w:sectPr. Rendering them as visual separators in a continuous layout is fine.
features.approach: Insert a break element; render as a horizontal divider/new-page marker; export as page break run or new sectPr. Continuous/odd/even/next-page section types stored in model.

features.name: Headers & footers (per-section, first-page-different, odd/even)
features.rationale: The DATA model (header1.xml/footer1.xml, first/even/default) round-trips fine. But rendering a real repeating header/footer on every printed page requires real pagination, which is infeasible here. You can show ONE editable header/footer region per section, not one-per-page.
features.approach: Provide editable header/footer zones bound to the section; round-trip the three header/footer types. Page-number fields in headers compute only at print/PDF time (see printing). Accept that on-screen they appear once, not per page.

features.name: Columns (newspaper-style multi-column)
features.rationale: CSS column-count/column-gap gives multi-column flow easily for display and maps to w:cols. But CSS columns + contenteditable have known caret/selection bugs, and balancing/column breaks won't match Word.
features.approach: Use CSS multicol for rendering and round-trip w:cols. Warn about editing quirks; column breaks stored in model and approximated.

features.name: Page background, watermark, page borders
features.rationale: Page color and a watermark image/text are easy as CSS background/overlay; round-trip to w:background and the header-drawing watermark is partial. Page borders (w:pgBorders) render as CSS border on the page container.
features.approach: CSS background + absolutely-positioned watermark overlay; border on page container. Export to background/pgBorders; watermark as header VML/DrawingML is the messy part - implement as best-effort.

features.name: Line numbers, vertical alignment of page content, gutter/mirror margins
features.rationale: Without real pagination, per-page line numbering and page-content vertical centering can't be done accurately; gutter/mirror margin is just a sectPr value to round-trip.
features.approach: Round-trip the properties; render line numbers per-paragraph as an approximation; skip true per-page vertical alignment.

features.name: Basic tables (insert, rows/cols, cell text, borders, shading, merge/split cells)
features.rationale: HTML <table> in contenteditable is well supported; OOXML w:tbl is verbose but standard and the 'docx' library handles it. Merge maps to gridSpan/vMerge.
features.approach: Use native HTML tables; build toolbar for insert/delete row-col, merge (rowspan/colspan), borders, shading. Map to w:tbl/w:tr/w:tc, w:tcBorders, w:shd, w:gridSpan/w:vMerge on export.

features.name: Cell sizing, autofit, table width modes, alignment
features.rationale: HTML table layout differs from Word's grid model; autofit-to-contents vs fixed widths approximately maps but exact column widths/twips won't match Word's rendering.
features.approach: Support fixed and percent widths (tblW, tcW); use table-layout:fixed for predictability; round-trip the grid (w:tblGrid). Exact autofit parity not guaranteed.

features.name: Nested tables, header rows repeating across pages, cell margins, text direction in cells
features.rationale: Nested tables and cell margins are fine. 'Repeat header row across pages' depends on pagination (infeasible to render, but tblHeader round-trips). Vertical text in cells is CSS writing-mode (approximate).
features.approach: Support nesting and cell margins directly; round-trip tblHeader; writing-mode for vertical text. No per-page header repetition rendering.

features.name: Formulas in table cells, sort, convert text<->table
features.rationale: Word table formulas (=SUM(ABOVE)) are simple field evaluations; sort and text<->table conversions are pure data transforms.
features.approach: Implement a small formula evaluator for SUM/AVERAGE/COUNT over cell ranges; text<->table via delimiter parsing; sort by column. Round-trip formulas as fields.

categories.area: Images, shapes, SmartArt, charts, icons, 3D models

features.name: Inline raster images (insert, resize, basic crop)
features.rationale: with base64/blob is trivial; OOXML inline drawing (a:blip + relationship + media part) is well supported by the 'docx' library.
features.approach: Insert , resize via handles, store bytes in the package media folder, emit the drawing XML + relationship. Crop via CSS clip on screen and srcRect on export.

features.name: Image wrapping (square/tight/through/behind/in front), text-wrap and absolute positioning
features.rationale: Inline and simple float (left/right) wrapping work with CSS float; 'tight'/'through' contour wrapping and absolute floating-over-text positioning are hard in flowing HTML and conflict with contenteditable.
features.approach: Support inline + float left/right (square wrap) well; round-trip anchor/wrap XML. Tight/through and free positioning: approximate with float or absolute overlay; full fidelity out of scope.

features.name: Shapes / text boxes (autoshapes, lines, connectors)
features.rationale: Simple shapes can be rendered as SVG/divs and round-tripped to DrawingML preset geometries, but the full preset-shape catalog, connectors, and adjust-handles are large.
features.approach: Render basic rectangles/ellipses/lines/text boxes as SVG overlays; map common presets (rect, ellipse, line) to a:prstGeom. Treat the long tail of shapes and connectors as out of scope.

features.name: Charts
features.rationale: A chart is an embedded chart part (chart1.xml in DrawingML chart schema) with a cached image. Rendering with a JS chart lib is easy; producing valid c:chart XML for round-trip is significant but bounded for common types.
features.approach: Render via Chart.js/ECharts from a data table; export common types (bar/line/pie) to the chart part with an embedded data cache + fallback image. Less-common chart types out of scope.

features.name: SmartArt
features.rationale: SmartArt is its own layout engine (diagram data + layout + style XML parts, dozens of layouts with constraint-based positioning). Re-implementing the SmartArt layout engine and its XML in one session is not realistic.
features.approach: Best you can offer: import SmartArt as a flattened static image/grouped shapes (read-only), and let users insert plain grouped shapes instead. No authoring of true SmartArt.

features.name: Icons (stock icon library)
features.rationale: Icons are just SVG images inserted as pictures. Provide a bundled SVG icon set.
features.approach: Ship an SVG icon picker; insert as image (rasterize or embed SVG-as-image). Round-trips as a normal picture/SVG blip.

features.name: 3D models (.glb/.fbx animated models)
features.rationale: Word's 3D model feature embeds 3D model parts with an interactive viewer (camera presets). Rendering needs WebGL/three.js (doable) but the OOXML 3D model packaging and Word-compatible round-trip is niche and not realistic in one session.
features.approach: At most, render an imported 3D model preview with three.js as read-only and substitute a static thumbnail on export. Not a real implementation.

features.name: Bulleted and numbered lists, indentation/demote/promote, restart/continue numbering
features.rationale: HTML ul/ol cover most cases; OOXML lists use numbering.xml (abstractNum/num + ilvl) which is verbose but standard.
features.approach: Render ul/ol or use the numbering model with CSS counters for fidelity; Tab/Shift-Tab to change level; map to numId/ilvl and numbering.xml on export. Restart/continue via separate num definitions.

features.name: Multilevel lists, custom number formats (1.1.1, legal, mixed bullet/number), list styles linked to headings
features.rationale: Multilevel numbering with custom formats and per-level text (e.g. '%1.%2.') requires faithfully modeling abstractNum levels; CSS counters can render most patterns but Word's full level-text/legal/leading-zero options need care.
features.approach: Model abstractNum levels with lvlText/numFmt; render via CSS counters with counter() concatenation. Cover decimal/roman/letter/bullet and dotted patterns; exotic formats partial. Round-trip numbering.xml.

features.name: Picture bullets, list number alignment/spacing
features.rationale: Picture bullets need an image as the marker (CSS list-style-image / ::marker), workable; precise number-position/text-indent parity with Word is approximate.
features.approach: list-style-image or pseudo-element marker; round-trip lvlPicBulletId. Spacing approximated.

features.name: Plain find & replace, case-sensitive, whole word, highlight all matches
features.rationale: Operate over the document text model with JS string search; highlight via the CSS Custom Highlight API or marker spans.
features.approach: Flatten model to text with offset map back to DOM ranges; search; render highlights via Highlight API; replace by splicing the model and re-rendering affected runs.

features.name: Wildcard / regex find & replace
features.rationale: JS RegExp covers Word's wildcard semantics; you translate Word wildcard syntax (e.g. ? * [ ] {n,m} \1 back-references) to RegExp.
features.approach: Build a Word-wildcard->RegExp translator; apply over the text model; support group back-references in replacement. Straightforward.

features.name: Find by formatting / style / special characters (paragraph marks, page breaks), Go To
features.rationale: Find-by-format and find special marks require searching over the rich model, not just text; doable but more plumbing. 'Go To page' depends on pagination (infeasible accurately).
features.approach: Extend search predicates to match run/paragraph properties and special-node types; Go To paragraph/bookmark works, Go To page only approximate.

features.name: Spell check with squiggles and suggestions
features.rationale: Two cheap paths: native contenteditable spellcheck=true (Chromium built-in), or a JS dictionary (Hunspell via WASM, e.g. nspell + dictionaries) for suggestions and custom dictionary.
features.approach: Enable native spellcheck for an instant MVP; for custom dictionaries/suggestions UI use nspell/hunspell-wasm over tokenized text, render squiggles via Highlight API, offer right-click suggestions.

features.name: Grammar checking / style critique (Editor pane)
features.rationale: Word's grammar engine is proprietary ML. You can integrate an open-source grammar checker (LanguageTool, self-hostable or as a library/server) for decent grammar/style, but parity with Microsoft Editor is not achievable.
features.approach: Optionally call a bundled/remote LanguageTool instance for grammar/style suggestions. Honest limitation: quality differs from Word.

features.name: Multi-language detection, readability stats, thesaurus
features.rationale: Language tagging and word-count/readability formulas (Flesch) are simple computations; thesaurus needs a dataset (WordNet).
features.approach: Compute readability/word stats directly; ship WordNet for thesaurus; language detection via a small JS lib. All approximate but feasible at small scope.

features.name: Comments (add, reply, resolve, anchored to range)
features.rationale: Comments are ranges + a sidebar; OOXML comments live in comments.xml with commentRangeStart/End markers - well supported.
features.approach: Store comment anchors as marker nodes around a range; render margin bubbles; threads/replies in model; round-trip comments.xml (+ commentsExtended for replies/resolve).

features.name: Track changes (record insertions/deletions/format changes with author/time, accept/reject)
features.rationale: Conceptually doable - wrap inserted runs in ins, mark deletions as del with strike rendering, store author/date. Maps to w:ins/w:del/w:rPrChange. The hard parts are correctly tracking EVERY edit (especially formatting and complex structural edits like table/row changes) through contenteditable's unpredictable mutations.
features.approach: Intercept all edits through a command layer (not raw contenteditable input) so every change is a tracked operation; render ins underlined/del struck; accept/reject mutates the model. Cover text + formatting changes well; structural change tracking (moved content, table edits) is partial.

features.name: Compare / combine documents (diff two docx)
features.rationale: A document diff over the parsed models is a tree/sequence diff problem; producing a Word-style redline merge is achievable for text but hard for structure.
features.approach: Parse both docx to a normalized model, run a sequence/tree diff (e.g. Myers on flattened runs), render as tracked changes. Good for prose, weaker on tables/structure.

categories.area: References (TOC, footnotes, citations, bibliography, captions, index)

features.name: Footnotes & endnotes
features.rationale: Data model round-trips well (footnotes.xml/endnotes.xml + footnoteReference). Rendering footnotes at the BOTTOM OF THE PAGE requires pagination (infeasible); without it, render them at end-of-document or in a panel.
features.approach: Store notes; render references as superscript links to a notes section at document end (or a hover panel); round-trip footnotes/endnotes XML. True page-bottom placement only at PDF/print stage if at all.

features.name: Table of contents (TOC)
features.rationale: Generating a TOC from heading styles is easy as a list of headings with links; the TOC is a field. Page NUMBERS in the TOC require pagination (infeasible accurately on screen).
features.approach: Build TOC from heading nodes with clickable anchors; export as a TOC field with cached entries; page numbers only meaningful in the PDF/print pass, otherwise omit or show '#'.

features.name: Captions, cross-references, table of figures
features.rationale: Captions are styled paragraphs with a SEQ field; cross-references are bookmarks+REF fields. Doable, but auto-renumbering and the page-number variants depend on field updates/pagination.
features.approach: Insert caption paragraphs with SEQ counters; cross-refs via bookmarks and REF fields; recompute numbers on edit. Page-number cross-refs limited by pagination.

features.name: Citations & bibliography (source manager, styles APA/MLA/Chicago)
features.rationale: This is pure data + formatting and there's mature open tooling (citation-js / CSL with citeproc-js + CSL style files). Independent of layout.
features.approach: Use citeproc-js + CSL styles for formatting citations and bibliography from a sources store; insert as fields; export bibliography as a paragraph block. Round-trip Word's bibliography part is partial but citations themselves work well.

features.name: Index (mark entries, generate index)
features.rationale: Marking XE entries and generating an alphabetized index is straightforward data work; index page numbers again need pagination.
features.approach: Mark index entries (XE fields), generate sorted index block. Page numbers limited; otherwise full feature works.

features.name: Mail merge (data source -> merge fields -> generated documents, letters/labels/envelopes)
features.rationale: Mail merge is data binding + iteration, no layout dependency. CSV/JSON/Excel data source + MERGEFIELD placeholders + generate N documents is a pure data task and a natural fit for JS.
features.approach: Parse data source (CSV via papaparse, XLSX via SheetJS); insert MERGEFIELD placeholders; on merge, clone the template model per record and substitute; export each as docx/PDF or a combined file. Rules (IF/NEXT fields) and label grids are extra but feasible. Envelopes/labels need page templates.

features.name: Equation editor (insert/edit math, professional rendering)
features.rationale: Rendering math is easy with MathJax/KaTeX. The friction is OOXML math (OMML, m:oMath) round-tripping: you must convert between your math representation and OMML. MathML<->OMML converters exist (XSLT/known libs) which makes this tractable but fiddly.
features.approach: Author via a MathLive/MathQuill editor producing MathML/LaTeX; render with MathJax; convert MathML<->OMML (existing XSLT) for round-trip. Common math covered; exotic constructs partial.

features.name: VBA macros (record, run, VBA project storage)
features.rationale: VBA requires a VBA language runtime and the Word object model (Application/Document/Selection/Range with hundreds of members). Implementing a VBA interpreter and the full object model in one session is unrealistic, and round-tripping the binary vbaProject.bin is its own challenge.
features.approach: Not realistic. The only honest alternative is a JavaScript-based scripting/macro API exposing a small subset of the document model (insert text, format selection) - a different, much smaller feature, NOT VBA compatibility. Existing VBA in opened files would be preserved as an opaque part if possible but not executed.

features.name: Office Add-ins (Office.js task panes / content add-ins / store)
features.rationale: Office Add-ins require implementing the Office.js host API surface and the add-in manifest/runtime that Microsoft defines; matching that contract so real store add-ins run is a massive, moving-target compatibility effort.
features.approach: Out of scope. A homegrown plugin system (your own JS extension API + sandboxed iframe panels) is feasible as a SEPARATE concept, but it would not run actual Microsoft Office add-ins.

features.name: Real-time multi-user co-authoring (live cursors, conflict-free merge)
features.rationale: The CRDT/OT machinery is feasible in JS (Yjs is mature - note this repo even references '.yrs'/Yrs). The blockers are scope (needs a server + auth + persistence + presence) and binding a CRDT cleanly to a rich contenteditable model. Not a same-session deliverable alongside everything else, but technically tractable.
features.approach: Use Yjs (Y.XmlFragment) bound to the editor with y-websocket and an awareness channel for cursors. Realistic only as a focused effort; full Word/OneDrive cloud parity (versioning, permissions) is out of scope for one session.

features.name: Cloud storage / autosave / version history integration
features.rationale: Saving to a cloud provider (e.g. Google Drive API, which is available in this environment) and keeping versions is feasible plumbing; matching Word+OneDrive's exact experience is not.
features.approach: Integrate a storage backend (local + one cloud provider) with periodic autosave and a simple version log. Keep expectations modest.

features.name: .docx read
features.rationale: Reading docx is feasible for a meaningful subset (text, runs, paragraphs, styles, tables, images, lists) using mammoth.js (->HTML) or docx-preview, or a custom OOXML parser. But OOXML is a huge spec; full-fidelity import of every feature (fields, complex sections, drawings, math, content controls) is not one-session.
features.approach: Use docx-preview/custom unzip+XML parse for import into the model; cover the common 80%; preserve unknown parts to re-emit on save (round-trip preservation) where possible.

features.name: .docx write
features.rationale: The 'docx' npm library generates valid docx for supported constructs. Mapping your full model back to OOXML is bounded but every feature you support needs its own serializer; advanced features won't serialize perfectly.
features.approach: Serialize model -> OOXML via the 'docx' lib (or hand-built XML + jszip). True round-trip fidelity (open Word file, edit, save, byte/semantic-stable) is the hardest correctness goal - aim for semantic preservation, not identity.

features.name: .doc (legacy binary Word 97-2003)
features.rationale: Legacy .doc is the binary CFB/FIB format - undocumented-feeling, hugely complex, with almost no maintained JS writer. Reading is partial at best (some libs extract text); writing is effectively unavailable in JS.
features.approach: At most extract text on import via a library; do not support saving .doc. Direct users to docx.

features.name: .rtf
features.rationale: RTF is a documented text-token format; basic read/write of text+formatting is feasible, full fidelity (every control word) is large.
features.approach: Implement an RTF reader/writer for common constructs (runs, paragraphs, tables, images). Treat exotic control words as out of scope.

features.name: .odt
features.rationale: ODT is a zipped XML format (like docx but the ODF schema). Feasible for a subset with effort; no single dominant JS library makes it as easy as docx.
features.approach: Parse/emit ODF XML for the common subset, reusing the same internal model. Lower priority than docx.

features.name: .pdf export
features.rationale: Exporting visible content to PDF is easy in Electron (webContents.printToPDF uses Chromium). The catch: it paginates with Chromium's print engine, not Word's, so page breaks/headers/footers/TOC page numbers won't match Word - and only this print path can realize repeating headers/footers and footnote-at-page-bottom.
features.approach: Use Electron printToPDF with a print stylesheet (@page, running headers/footers via CSS, page counters). This is also where header/footer/TOC-page-number features become real (Chromium-paginated, not Word-identical). PDF import/editing is out of scope.

features.name: Print to physical printer with options (range, copies, scaling, duplex)
features.rationale: Electron exposes webContents.print with the OS print dialog/options; this works. But what prints is the Chromium-paginated layout, not a Word-faithful page model, and advanced options (booklet, manual duplex) depend on OS/driver.
features.approach: Use webContents.print with a dedicated print CSS; expose range/copies/scale; rely on the OS dialog for driver options. Print preview = the PDF render. Pagination fidelity caveat applies.

features.name: Authoring-side: alt text, heading structure, accessibility checker
features.rationale: Capturing alt text on images, enforcing heading structure, and running a rule-based accessibility checker (missing alt, contrast, heading order, table headers) are simple data/lint passes; round-trip alt text to docDescr/descr.
features.approach: Alt-text field on media; an accessibility-checker that lints the model against WCAG-like rules; export alt text into OOXML drawing properties.

features.name: App-side accessibility (screen reader support for the editor UI/content)
features.rationale: contenteditable has baseline AT support, but making a custom rich editor truly screen-reader friendly (ARIA, navigation, announcing formatting) is significant, ongoing work and rarely fully solved even by mature web editors.
features.approach: Add ARIA roles/labels to chrome and content regions, keyboard navigation, focus management. Achieve basic usability; full parity with Word's MSAA/UIA accessibility is out of scope.

features.name: Read Aloud (text-to-speech with highlighting)
features.rationale: Browser SpeechSynthesis API (available in Electron/Chromium) reads text and fires boundary events for word highlighting.
features.approach: Walk the text model, feed SpeechSynthesisUtterance, use onboundary events to highlight the current word via the Highlight API. Quick win.

features.name: Dictation (speech-to-text into the document)
features.rationale: The Web Speech Recognition API exists but in Electron/Chromium it typically needs a cloud backend and is less reliable; quality and offline support vary. Voice commands (formatting by voice) add scope.
features.approach: Use Web Speech Recognition or integrate a cloud STT (e.g. via an API); insert transcribed text at the caret. Treat punctuation/voice-command parity with Word's dictation as partial.

features.name: Document encryption (password to open) compatible with Word
features.rationale: Word's docx encryption uses MS-OFFCRYPTO (Agile/Standard encryption inside a CFB container with ECMA-376 specifics). Reading password-protected files needs a lib; WRITING Word-compatible encrypted docx in pure JS is uncommon and intricate.
features.approach: For decryption use an existing MS-OFFCRYPTO JS implementation if available; for your own at-rest protection, encrypt the package with WebCrypto (not Word-openable). Word-compatible write-encryption is effectively out of scope for one session.

features.name: Restrict editing / formatting restrictions / mark as final / read-only
features.rationale: App-enforced read-only, editing-region restrictions, and 'mark as final' are easy in your own app and round-trip to documentProtection settings - but enforcement is only honored by your app, exactly as Word's is only soft-enforced without IRM.
features.approach: Implement editing restrictions in the editor (toggle editability, permitted regions), round-trip w:documentProtection. Honest note: it's advisory, not cryptographic.

features.name: Digital signatures, IRM/sensitivity labels
features.rationale: Digital signatures (XML-DSig over the package) and IRM/Azure Information Protection require certificate/PKI and Microsoft rights-management services integration - far beyond a one-session scope.
features.approach: Out of scope. Possibly preserve existing signature parts opaquely, but neither create nor validate them.

recommendations.topic: Editor core: contenteditable+execCommand vs ProseMirror/TipTap vs Lexical vs Quill
recommendations.recommendation: For a one-session, no-bundler build in an Electron renderer with nodeIntegration + raw require(), pick ProseMirror core (prosemirror-model 1.x, prosemirror-state 1.x, prosemirror-view 1.41.x, prosemirror-transform, prosemirror-schema-basic, prosemirror-commands, prosemirror-keymap, prosemirror-history) loaded via require(). It is the only credible Word-fidelity option whose packages I empirically confirmed require() cleanly in plain CommonJS with zero bundler. Second choice for speed-of-build is raw contenteditable + a thin command layer (NOT document.execCommand). Avoid TipTap, Lexical-as-primary, and Quill for this specific constraint set.
recommendations.rationale: I verified module formats against the live npm registry and then actually ran require() in a clean Node CJS sandbox (mirrors Electron renderer w/ nodeIntegration doing bare require). Results: require('prosemirror-model'/'prosemirror-state'/'prosemirror-view') => OK; require('lexical') => OK (0.45.0 ships dist/Lexical.js CJS main); require('quill') => FAIL with ERR_REQUIRE_ESM (Quill 2.0.3 is now an ESM-only package — its require entry is gone; you can only get it via the UMD dist/quill.js script tag, not require). ProseMirror gives a real document model + schema + transactions, which is what Word fidelity (styles, marks, block structure, tables via prosemirror-tables) actually needs; you serialize to/from HTML with DOMSerializer/DOMParser which dovetails with the mammoth import and html-to-docx export paths. document.execCommand is officially deprecated (MDN/WHATWG), behaves inconsistently, produces garbage nested spans, and has no undo/redo model you can trust — it is a dead end for anything Word-like. Lexical IS require()-able and is excellent, but its real power is the @lexical/react binding + a constellation of @lexical/* plugin packages; using core-only by hand in one session is more friction than ProseMirror core, and its node/transform API churns between 0.x releases.
libraries: quill@2.0.3 (ESM-only package: use UMD dist/quill.js via <script>, NOT require)
pitfalls: Quill 2.0.3 cannot be require()'d (ERR_REQUIRE_ESM confirmed empirically) — if you insist on Quill, drop the UMD build in via a <script> tag and use the global, do not require() it. Also Quill has no native tables in 2.0 and its Delta model is lossy vs Word.

document.execCommand is deprecated and non-deterministic across selections/browsers; do not build the editor on it. If you go raw-contenteditable, implement formatting via Range/Selection + your own command + undo stack, not execCommand.

TipTap is technically ProseMirror under the hood and @tiptap/core@3.24.0 / @tiptap/pm resolve CJS, but TipTap's idiomatic usage (extensions, @tiptap/react|vue) assumes a build step; using it without a bundler fights the framework. If you want ProseMirror, use ProseMirror directly.

ProseMirror in the renderer: bare require('prosemirror-view') resolves from node_modules only because nodeIntegration gives the renderer Node's resolver — this works, but each prosemirror-* is a separate package (no single bundle), so you require ~9 packages individually.
Lexical's public node/transform APIs are explicitly unstable across 0.x; pin the exact version (0.45.0) and don't expect API stability.
Whatever you pick, the editor model is NOT the page-layout model — see pagination topic; the editor produces a single logical flow, pagination is a separate rendering concern.

recommendations.topic: WYSIWYG pagination: discrete fixed-size pages (US Letter/A4) with margins from a contenteditable flow
recommendations.recommendation: Do NOT try to make contenteditable itself paginate. Use a two-layer model: (1) a single continuous editable flow (the ProseMirror doc / one contenteditable) as the source of truth; (2) a separate non-editable 'page view' renderer that measures the flow and slices it into fixed-size .page elements (US Letter 8.5x11in = 816x1056px @96dpi; A4 = 794x1123px) with CSS padding for margins, styled with box-shadow on a gray canvas. For the one-session build, implement measurement-based pagination: render content into a hidden page-height box, walk block children, and emit a page break (move to next .page) when accumulated height exceeds the content box height. Drive print/export off CSS @page + page-break-before, not off the on-screen slicing.
recommendations.rationale: Confirmed by the discuss.prosemirror 'Paginated Editing' thread and multiple write-ups (Wax/Hacker News, TOAST UI) that true in-place pagination of contenteditable is effectively a 'near impossible' DOM problem and is why Google Docs/Zoho render their own layout out of the browser's control. The DOM gives you no layout state (you cannot ask 'where does line 3 end' without expensive Range.getBoundingClientRect measurements), and CSS page-break-* is a print-time construct, not an interactive one. The pragmatic, achievable-in-one-session approach is the measure-and-slice page view: fixed pixel page dimensions + getBoundingClientRect height accumulation per block to decide breaks. This is the approach paginated-ProseMirror plugins and 'A4 contenteditable' demos converge on.
libraries: No mature drop-in npm lib is reliable here — hand-roll it.
Reference approaches: prosemirror-pagination-style community plugins (treat as reference, not dependency)
CSS only: @page { size: Letter; margin: 1in } + .page { width:816px; min-height:1056px; padding:96px } for the 1in-margin US Letter case
pitfalls: Editing inside sliced pages breaks the editor: keep ONE editable flow as source of truth and re-paginate into read-only page DOM, or accept jank. Trying to make each .page independently contenteditable creates cursor-jump and selection-across-pages nightmares.

Splitting a block (paragraph/table row) across a page boundary mid-element is the genuinely hard part — naive block-level slicing leaves big gaps at page bottoms. Word splits lines within a paragraph; replicating that needs line-box measurement, which is slow and fiddly.

px<->inch is dpi-dependent. Use 96px/in for screen, but the printed PDF uses real inches via @page — keep the two coordinate systems separate and don't expect pixel-perfect parity between screen pagination and printed pagination.
Re-pagination on every keystroke is expensive (forced layout/reflow from getBoundingClientRect). Debounce, and paginate incrementally from the changed block forward.
Tables, images, and headers/footers each need special break handling; for a one-session build, scope to paragraph/heading/list flow first.

On-screen page breaks will NOT match print breaks unless you drive both from the same CSS; prefer letting Chromium do print pagination via @page for the actual PDF rather than reusing your screen slices.

recommendations.recommendation: Import: mammoth@1.12.0 (require('mammoth').convertToHtml) — confirmed require()-able CJS. Export: pick by need. For an HTML-in/docx-out pipeline that matches your contenteditable content with the least code, use html-to-docx@1.8.0 (TurboDocx fork; require() returns a function — confirmed). For maximum fidelity/control of the output document, use docx@9.7.1 (require('docx') OK via dist/index.umd.cjs) and build the document programmatically from your model. Avoid html-docx-js for cross-app compatibility. Treat .docx round-trip as lossy by design and do not expect byte-perfect fidelity.
recommendations.rationale: Versions confirmed against npm registry and require() confirmed in a clean CJS sandbox: mammoth 1.12.0 (lib/index.js CJS, BSD-2), html-to-docx 1.8.0 (UMD main, require => function, MIT), docx 9.7.1 (UMD cjs main + actively maintained, MIT). mammoth is the de-facto docx->HTML importer; it intentionally targets semantic HTML driven by Word styles, which pairs well with a ProseMirror schema. html-to-docx is explicitly an html-docx-js successor that fixes the altchunks compatibility problem (html-docx-js wraps HTML in an altChunk that Google Docs/LibreOffice render poorly), so it's the better HTML->docx converter. docx is the gold standard when you control the model and want real Word constructs (sections, styles, headers/footers, page size/margins, tables) rather than HTML-guessed output.
libraries: html-to-docx@1.8.0 (export HTML->docx; require OK; TurboDocx fork)
docx@9.7.1 (export model->docx, highest fidelity; require OK via UMD cjs)
html-docx-js@0.3.1 (avoid: altChunks => poor LibreOffice/Google Docs compatibility)
pitfalls: Round-trip is fundamentally lossy: docx and HTML have mismatched models. mammoth's own docs say complex docs won't convert perfectly and it works best when the source uses Word styles semantically.
mammoth does NO sanitization of source docx — dangerous with untrusted files; fine for a single-user trusted local app but never on untrusted input.
mammoth output is an HTML fragment (no <meta charset>), so non-ASCII can render wrong if you drop it into a file without forcing UTF-8.
mammoth's document-transform API is explicitly unstable across versions — pin 1.12.0.
mammoth drops/approximates tables, embedded media, columns, text boxes, and exact spacing; you lose layout, keep structure.
html-to-docx maps a constrained subset of CSS — complex CSS (flex/grid, absolute positioning, custom fonts) won't translate; style with simple block HTML + inline-ish CSS it understands.
html-docx-js uses Word's altChunks feature: Word opens it but Google Docs/LibreOffice render it badly — this is the specific reason to prefer html-to-docx.
For best fidelity, don't HTML-convert at all on export: serialize your editor model straight into docx@9.7.1 constructs (Paragraph/TextRun/Table/SectionProperties with pageSize+margins).
html-to-docx 1.8.0 has a long-stale changelog history; validate output in real Word/LibreOffice before shipping.

recommendations.topic: Print to physical printer / PDF in Electron
recommendations.recommendation: Use webContents.printToPDF(options) for 'Save as PDF' and webContents.print(options, cb) for physical printing. For correct US Letter/A4 page geometry, set pageSize ('Letter'/'A4' or an object with width/height in MICRONS), margins (the modern object form {top,bottom,left,right} in inches, or the legacy marginsType), printBackground:true (so page shadows/fills/colors appear), and landscape as needed. Add displayHeaderFooter + headerTemplate/footerTemplate for page numbers (use the .pageNumber/.totalPages/.title/.date/.url injected classes). Print from a hidden/offscreen BrowserWindow that loads a print-specific stylesheet with @page rules, rather than printing the on-screen paginated editor.
recommendations.rationale: Confirmed against current Electron webContents docs/issues: printToPDF accepts pageSize (named or microns), landscape, margins, printBackground, pageRanges ('1-5, 8, 11-13'), displayHeaderFooter, headerTemplate, footerTemplate (with class hooks date/title/url/pageNumber/totalPages). printToPDF returns a Promise<Buffer> in modern Electron. Driving print off a dedicated print stylesheet + @page lets Chromium's print engine handle pagination correctly, which is far more reliable than reusing the screen-sliced pages.
libraries: Use latest Electron stable (Electron 3x line, e.g. 35/36+ in 2025/2026); printToPDF header/footer support is in current versions
pitfalls: landscape is IGNORED if the page uses a CSS @page rule — let @page own size/orientation OR use the option, not both conflictingly.
printBackground defaults to false: without it, page backgrounds/box-shadows/fills won't print, so the PDF won't match the editor.

headerTemplate/footerTemplate are finicky: they render at a tiny default font, need explicit inline font-size, and the document body margins must leave room or header/footer overlap content — a long-standing source of bugs.
Custom pageSize is in microns (1 inch = 25400 microns), NOT pixels — a frequent mistake that yields wrong-sized pages.
Print the actual document at print DPI via @page, not your 96dpi screen slices; screen pagination and print pagination can differ in line/page breaks.
marginsType is the older API (0 default / 1 none / 2 minimum); the object margins form is clearer — pick one and be consistent.
webContents.print silent printing/printer selection options vary; if you need a specific printer or silent print, pass deviceName/silent and test on the target OS.

recommendations.topic: Electron security posture for a local, trusted, single-user app
recommendations.recommendation: Recognize the explicit tradeoff: the OFFICIALLY recommended secure baseline is contextIsolation:true, nodeIntegration:false, sandbox:true, with Node access only via a preload script using contextBridge.exposeInMainWorld. For your stated one-session constraint (nodeIntegration + bare require() of CJS npm packages in the renderer), you are deliberately running the INSECURE-by-Electron's-standards config. That is defensible ONLY because: single user, no remote content, only local packaged files loaded, no untrusted input rendered. If you accept that scope, ship nodeIntegration:true + contextIsolation:false to get require() in the renderer for the fast build. The moment the app loads any remote URL, opens untrusted .docx-derived HTML without sanitization, or gains multi-user/network features, migrate to the secure baseline (preload + contextBridge) and move docx/mammoth/file IO into the main process behind IPC.
recommendations.rationale: Confirmed from Electron's official security tutorial and the long-running defaults discussion (issue #23506): contextIsolation has been on by default since Electron 12 and is recommended for all apps; true isolation requires contextIsolation:true alongside nodeIntegration:false; the canonical pattern exposes only audited functions via contextBridge. The risk that nodeIntegration:true mitigates against is remote/untrusted content escalating to full Node/OS access — a threat that essentially doesn't exist for a single-user app that only ever loads its own bundled local HTML and never renders untrusted markup. So the tradeoff is real and the relaxed posture is justifiable within that narrow envelope, with a clear migration trigger.
libraries: Electron contextBridge + ipcMain/ipcRenderer (the secure path, if/when you migrate)
No third-party security lib required; rely on Electron BrowserWindow webPreferences
pitfalls: nodeIntegration:true + contextIsolation:false means ANY script the renderer executes has full Node/OS access — one injected/untrusted string of HTML (e.g. from an imported .docx via mammoth, which does NO sanitization) becomes RCE. This is the single biggest risk and directly intersects the docx-import topic.
If you ever render imported/Word HTML, sanitize it (e.g. DOMPurify) regardless of posture — mammoth explicitly does not sanitize.
Don't half-migrate: contextIsolation:true with nodeIntegration:true is a known foot-gun; pick a coherent posture.

Set webPreferences explicitly — defaults changed across Electron majors (contextIsolation default true since 12, sandbox default true since 20), so relying on defaults gives different behavior per Electron version.
If you relax security for the build, isolate it: never set this window to load external URLs, set a strict CSP, and keep navigation locked to local files (will-navigate / setWindowOpenHandler).

Plan the IPC seam now even if you ship the relaxed config: keep file open/save, docx import (mammoth) and export (docx/html-to-docx) in functions you can later move to the main process behind contextBridge with minimal refactor.
